threats.title: Bot-Initiated Yield Liquidation Path Completely Broken Due to Wrong Yield Account Resolution
threats.hypothesis_description: In YieldStakingBase._unstake and _repay, the yield account is resolved via yieldAccounts[msg.sender]. When botAdmin calls these functions to force-liquidate unhealthy positions, the lookup uses the bot's address instead of the NFT owner's, resolving to address(0) or the wrong account. This causes reverts on accountYieldShares subtraction (underflow), protocolRequestWithdrawal/protocolClaimWithdraw operating on wrong accounts, and convertToYieldAssets returning incorrect values. The entire bot-forced liquidation mechanism is non-functional.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental code logic error affecting the protocol's safety mechanism for liquidating unhealthy yield positions. Without functional bot liquidation, positions that fall below the health factor threshold accumulate bad debt.

proof_of_signal.pattern: vars.yieldAccout = IYieldAccount(yieldAccounts[msg.sender]) resolves to bot's account, not sd.yieldAccount; accountYieldShares[address(vars.yieldAccout)] -= sd.yieldShare will underflow

preconditions: A yield stake position exists with health factor below unstakeHeathFactor
botAdmin is configured and attempts to call unstake/repay
botAdmin has no yield account or a different one than the NFT owner

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: yieldAccounts[msg.sender] used uniformly regardless of whether caller is owner or bot
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Bot liquidation is the only mechanism to force-close unhealthy yield positions
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - any position that becomes unhealthy cannot be bot-liquidated

operational_questions: Can any yield positions currently be bot-liquidated?
What is the accumulated bad debt from unliquidatable positions?
Do protocolRequestWithdrawal implementations also use yieldAccounts[msg.sender]?
validation_requirements: Test bot calling unstake on a position - verify revert
Test bot calling repay on an unstaked position - verify revert
Verify that all yield protocol implementations (Lido, Etherfi, sDAI) use msg.sender for account lookup

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Stake an NFT with maximum leverage (up to leverageFactor of NFT value)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for NFT price to drop or debt to accrue until HF < unstakeHeathFactor
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Bot attempts unstake but reverts due to wrong account lookup
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Position accumulates bad debt indefinitely, draining lending pool liquidity
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - any user can create positions that become unliquidatable by design

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Bad debt accumulation in lending pool, potential insolvency
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix account resolution logic
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All yield staking liquidations across Lido, Etherfi, and sDAI strategies

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic - just stake and wait for price movement

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Yield positions with HF < threshold that haven't been liquidated
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users maximizing leverage knowing bot can't liquidate

threats.title: Yield Borrow Cap Check Uses Wrong Index Allowing Excess Borrowing Beyond Intended Limits
threats.hypothesis_description: In YieldLogic.executeYieldBorrowERC20, the total supply for yield cap validation is calculated using groupData.borrowIndex instead of assetData.supplyIndex: `vars.totalSupply = VaultLogic.erc20GetTotalCrossSupply(assetData, groupData.borrowIndex)`. Since borrowIndex grows faster than supplyIndex (due to protocol fees and interest spread), the calculated totalSupply is inflated, making the yield cap percentage check more permissive than intended. This allows yield managers to borrow more liquidity than the configured percentage of actual deposits.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct calculation error in a critical cap enforcement mechanism. Over time, as the spread between borrowIndex and supplyIndex grows, the excess borrowing capacity increases proportionally.

proof_of_signal.pattern: VaultLogic.erc20GetTotalCrossSupply(assetData, groupData.borrowIndex) uses borrowIndex instead of assetData.supplyIndex

preconditions: Yield is enabled for the pool and asset
Non-zero feeFactor creates spread between supply and borrow indices
Time has passed since pool creation for index divergence to accumulate

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: erc20GetTotalCrossSupply is parameterized with an index - the wrong one is passed
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Yield cap is configured by admin but enforcement logic is flawed
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Grows over time as index spread increases

operational_questions: What is the current spread between borrowIndex and supplyIndex for yield-enabled assets?
How much excess borrowing capacity does this provide?
Could this drain available liquidity below safe levels?
validation_requirements: Calculate actual vs reported totalSupply at current index values
Test yield borrow at cap boundary with correct vs incorrect index
Quantify excess borrowing allowed per unit of index spread

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify yield-enabled assets with significant borrowIndex/supplyIndex divergence
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Borrow up to the inflated cap - more than intended percentage of actual deposits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: This excess borrowing reduces available liquidity below intended safety margins
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Regular depositors may be unable to withdraw if liquidity is over-borrowed
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Must be a whitelisted yield manager with yieldCap > 0

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Available liquidity drained below safe levels
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code fix; excess borrows must be repaid
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All yield borrowing across all strategies

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: More impactful as protocol ages and index spread grows

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Yield borrows approaching or exceeding intended cap percentage of actual supply
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Available liquidity dropping below expected floor
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Yield managers borrowing maximum allowed amounts

threats.title: Bot-Initiated Repay Reverts When Claimed Yield Insufficient to Cover Debt Plus Fine
threats.hypothesis_description: In YieldStakingBase._repay, when claimedYield < nftDebtWithFine, the function attempts `underlyingAsset.safeTransferFrom(vars.nftOwner, address(this), vars.extraAmount)` to pull the deficit from the NFT owner. When botAdmin calls repay for liquidation, this will revert if the NFT owner has not approved the yield staking contract for the underlying asset or has insufficient balance. This creates a permanent DoS on the repay path for bot-initiated liquidations, compounding the OPS-H001 issue.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even if OPS-H001 were fixed, this secondary DoS vector would block bot liquidations whenever the yield return is insufficient to cover the accumulated debt plus fine. A malicious user can intentionally ensure this condition is met.

proof_of_signal.pattern: underlyingAsset.safeTransferFrom(vars.nftOwner, address(this), vars.extraAmount) called when bot is msg.sender but pulling from nftOwner who may not have approved

preconditions: Yield position has been unstaked (state == UNSTAKE)
Claimed yield from protocol is less than debt + unstakeFine
NFT owner has not approved underlying asset to yield staking contract or has zero balance
botAdmin attempts to call repay

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: safeTransferFrom pulls from nftOwner regardless of who calls repay
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Bot liquidation is critical safety mechanism
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time yield returns are negative or insufficient to cover debt growth

operational_questions: Can the bot repay without pulling extra from the user?
What happens to the protocol debt when repay cannot complete?
Can users deliberately create positions designed to fail on repay?
validation_requirements: Test bot calling repay with zero user approval
Test with user having insufficient balance
Verify whether bad debt accumulates if repay always reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create yield stake position with maximum leverage
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Revoke any ERC20 approval to the yield staking contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for position to become unhealthy - NFT price drops or debt accrues
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Bot calls unstake (if OPS-H001 fixed), then repay reverts on safeTransferFrom

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Bot liquidation completion permanently blocked for these positions
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Bad debt accumulation in lending pool
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade or admin intervention
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All bot-initiated repayments where yield < debt + fine

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unstaked positions that never get repaid
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users with zero approval to yield staking contracts

threats.title: PoolManager Dispatch Delegatecalls to Address(0) When Module Uninstalled But Proxy Still Trusted
threats.hypothesis_description: In PoolManager.dispatch(), if a module is uninstalled (moduleLookup[moduleId] = address(0)) but the proxy remains in trustedSenders, the resolved moduleImpl becomes address(0). A delegatecall to address(0) succeeds silently with empty return data, causing user transactions to appear successful while performing no state changes. This could lead to users believing deposits/borrows/withdrawals succeeded when they did not, creating accounting discrepancies.
threats.investigation_rationale: The dispatch function has no null check on moduleImpl after resolution. This is a defensive programming gap that becomes exploitable if admin module management creates inconsistent state between trustedSenders and moduleLookup.
deployment_assumptions: Module uninstallation removes moduleLookup entry but may not remove trustedSenders entries for all proxies
threats.config_evidence: PoolManager.sol dispatch function - no require(moduleImpl != address(0)) after resolution

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (moduleImpl == address(0)) moduleImpl = moduleLookup[moduleId]; followed by delegatecall without null check

preconditions: A module has been uninstalled (moduleLookup[moduleId] set to address(0))
The module's proxy still exists in trustedSenders with moduleImpl = address(0)
User calls the stale proxy expecting normal operation

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No validation that resolved moduleImpl is non-zero before delegatecall
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Module lifecycle management must keep trustedSenders synchronized
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between module uninstall and proxy cleanup

operational_questions: Does the installer module properly clean up trustedSenders when uninstalling?
What happens to in-flight user transactions during module upgrades?
Could delegatecall to address(0) cause any side effects beyond silent success?
validation_requirements: Review installer module logic for proxy cleanup on uninstall
Test delegatecall to address(0) behavior with the specific calldata patterns used
Verify whether msg.value forwarded to address(0) is recoverable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe module uninstallation or upgrade transaction on-chain
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Before proxy cleanup (if any), call the stale proxy with deposit/borrow calldata
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction succeeds silently - tokens transferred to PoolManager but no accounting update
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Tokens become permanently locked in PoolManager with no supply/borrow records
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires monitoring admin module management transactions

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Users could lose deposited funds with no accounting record
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Silent failures could propagate through dependent operations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Emergency admin ether/token transfer could recover some funds
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All operations routed through the affected proxy

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium - requires timing around module upgrades

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Successful transactions with no state changes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Token balances in PoolManager exceeding tracked availableLiquidity
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Transactions to stale proxies after module upgrades

threats.title: Collected Unstake Fines Permanently Locked in Yield Staking Contracts With No Withdrawal Mechanism
threats.hypothesis_description: When botAdmin force-unstakes a position, an unstakeFine is set on the stake data. During _repay, this fine is collected (either from claimed yield or pulled from the NFT owner). The fine amount stays in the YieldStakingBase contract as underlying asset (WETH or DAI) but there is no function to withdraw these accumulated fines. The totalUnstakeFine tracker is only ever incremented (in _unstake) but never decremented (in _repay), and there's no sweep function for the underlying asset. The emergencyEtherTransfer only handles native ETH, not WETH/ERC20.
threats.investigation_rationale: Protocol revenue from unstake fines is permanently lost. As more positions are liquidated, the amount of locked funds grows monotonically.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalUnstakeFine only incremented, never decremented; no ERC20 sweep function exists; fine amount stays in contract after repay

preconditions: botAdmin successfully force-unstakes a position with non-zero unstakeFine
Repay completes collecting the fine
Protocol needs to recover the accumulated fines

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: totalUnstakeFine monotonically increases; underlying asset balance in contract includes unrecoverable fines
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin function to withdraw accumulated fines
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - grows with every bot liquidation

operational_questions: How much in fines has been collected and is now locked?
Can the YieldRegistry.rescue function be used to recover ERC20 tokens from YieldStakingBase?
Is there any upgrade path to add a sweep function?
validation_requirements: Verify no existing function can extract WETH/DAI from yield staking contracts
Check if emergencyEtherTransfer covers the native ETH case but not wrapped tokens
Confirm totalUnstakeFine is never decremented in any code path

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an attack per se - this is a permanent fund lock in protocol design
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Fines accumulate over time as positions are liquidated
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Protocol cannot recover these funds without contract upgrade
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If significant, represents material protocol revenue loss

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Accounting mismatch between tracked and actual fines
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to add ERC20 sweep function
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All yield staking strategies (Lido, Etherfi, sDAI)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: WETH/DAI balance in yield staking contracts exceeding zero when no active positions

threats.title: Asset Operations Bricked When Yield Group Enabled Without Rate Model Configuration
threats.hypothesis_description: When executeSetAssetYieldEnable is called to enable yield for an asset, it adds the yield group to assetData.groupList but does NOT set the rateModel (left as address(0)). The separate executeSetAssetYieldRate must be called afterward. Between these two calls, ANY operation that triggers InterestLogic.updateInterestRates (deposits, withdrawals, borrows, repays) will iterate over all groups including the new yield group and call IInterestRateModel(address(0)).calculateGroupBorrowRate(), which reverts. This bricks the entire asset until the rate model is set.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a configuration ordering dependency that creates a DoS window. If any user transaction occurs between yield enable and rate model configuration, the asset becomes temporarily frozen.
deployment_assumptions: Admin must call setAssetYieldRate in the same block or before any user interaction
threats.config_evidence: ConfigureLogic.sol executeSetAssetYieldEnable adds group without setting rateModel; InterestLogic.sol updateInterestRates unconditionally calls rateModel for all groups

proof_of_signal.pattern: groupData.groupId = poolData.yieldGroup; InterestLogic.initGroupData(groupData); but rateModel never set; updateInterestRates calls IInterestRateModel(loopGroupData.rateModel).calculateGroupBorrowRate() for all groups

preconditions: Pool admin calls setAssetYieldEnable(true) for an asset
setAssetYieldRate has NOT yet been called for this asset's yield group
Any user transaction triggers updateInterestRates on this asset

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Two-step configuration process with dangerous window between steps
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin must batch both calls atomically to avoid DoS
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between yield enable and rate model configuration transactions

operational_questions: Are admin configuration calls batched atomically in deployment scripts?
Can a user transaction slip between the two admin calls?
Does the DoS affect all asset operations or just rate-dependent ones?
validation_requirements: Test enabling yield without setting rate model, then attempt deposit
Verify updateInterestRates iterates over yield group even with zero borrows
Check if L2 sequencer ordering guarantees protect against user txs between admin calls

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor mempool/sequencer for admin setAssetYieldEnable call (limited on L2)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Submit any transaction touching the same asset before setAssetYieldRate
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All subsequent transactions on this asset revert until rate model is set
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users cannot deposit, withdraw, borrow, or repay on the affected asset
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires transaction between two admin calls - more feasible on L1

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Entire asset frozen for all operations
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users locked from all interactions with the asset
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Admin sets rate model to immediately fix; but any pending liquidations may fail
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All deposit/withdraw/borrow/repay operations on affected asset

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium - requires timing between admin calls

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Assets with yield group in groupList but rateModel == address(0)
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Reverts on standard operations after yield configuration

threats.title: Chainlink Oracle Lacks Time-Based Staleness Check Allowing Stale Prices in All Protocol Operations
threats.hypothesis_description: PriceOracle.getAssetPriceFromChainlink validates only `updatedAt != 0` and `answeredInRound >= roundId` but has no maximum age check (e.g., `block.timestamp - updatedAt < STALENESS_THRESHOLD`). If a Chainlink aggregator experiences a heartbeat failure or network congestion, stale prices persist indefinitely. Attackers can exploit the stale high price to borrow maximum amounts against overvalued collateral, or exploit stale low prices to trigger unfair liquidations.
threats.investigation_rationale: This protocol-specific implementation lacks the standard staleness threshold check that most Aave-fork derivatives include. The protocol explicitly uses multiple Chainlink feeds for different asset types, amplifying the risk surface.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(updatedAt != 0) and require(answeredInRound >= roundId) present; no require(block.timestamp - updatedAt < MAX_STALENESS)

preconditions: A Chainlink aggregator stops updating (heartbeat failure, network issues)
Real market price diverges significantly from last oracle update
Protocol continues using the stale price for all operations

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing time-based staleness validation in oracle price query
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No per-asset staleness configuration mechanism exists
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Duration of oracle downtime - could be hours

operational_questions: What are the heartbeat intervals for each configured Chainlink feed?
Has any configured aggregator historically experienced extended downtime?
What is the maximum asset price volatility within typical heartbeat windows?
validation_requirements: Catalog all Chainlink aggregators used and their heartbeat intervals
Simulate oracle downtime scenario with price divergence
Calculate maximum extractable value during a staleness window

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor Chainlink feeds for staleness (updatedAt significantly behind)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If collateral price has dropped in reality but oracle still shows old high price, deposit collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Borrow maximum amount against overvalued collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: When oracle updates, position is instantly underwater - bad debt for protocol
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - opportunistic exploitation during oracle downtime

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Price integrity of all oracle-dependent operations
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Bad debt from under-collateralized borrows; unfair liquidations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Bad debt cannot be recovered once created
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All borrowing, liquidation, yield staking health factor checks

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large borrows immediately after extended oracle silence
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Deposits and borrows concentrated after oracle staleness events

threats.title: YieldSavingsDai getAccountTotalUnstakedYield Underflow Causes DoS When sDAI Value Drops
threats.hypothesis_description: In YieldSavingsDai, getAccountTotalUnstakedYield calculates `balance - inWithdraw` where balance = sdai.balanceOf(account) and inWithdraw = accountYieldInWithdraws[account]. The inWithdraw value is set at unstake time based on current share value. If the sDAI exchange rate decreases (e.g., MakerDAO reduces DSR), the sDAI balance could become less than the recorded inWithdraw amount, causing an arithmetic underflow revert in Solidity 0.8+. This blocks all new stake operations for any NFT using that yield account, since _stake calls _convertToYieldSharesWithTotalYield which calls getAccountTotalUnstakedYield.
threats.investigation_rationale: While DSR decreases are rare, the code has no safety check for this condition. The explicit require in getAccountTotalUnstakedYield makes this a hard revert rather than a graceful degradation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(balance >= inWithdraw); return balance - inWithdraw; will revert if sDAI value drops below recorded withdrawal amount

preconditions: User has positions in UNSTAKE state with recorded withdrawAmount
sDAI exchange rate decreases (DSR reduction or loss event)
sdai.balanceOf(account) < accountYieldInWithdraws[account]

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hard require on balance >= inWithdraw without handling external value changes
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin mechanism to reset accountYieldInWithdraws
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any sDAI value reduction event

operational_questions: Has MakerDAO ever reduced DSR, causing sDAI value decrease?
Could a sDAI depeg event trigger this condition?
Can affected users still complete repay for unstaked positions?
validation_requirements: Simulate sDAI value drop with active unstaked positions
Verify whether _repay path is also blocked or only new stakes
Test conversion functions with balance < inWithdraw

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe sDAI exchange rate decrease (external event, not attacker-controlled)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Any yield account with unstaked positions has getAccountTotalUnstakedYield revert
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: New stakes to that account are blocked, preventing portfolio rebalancing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Existing positions may also be affected if share calculations rely on this function
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - triggered by external market event

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Yield account operations blocked for affected users
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Cannot create new positions or properly value existing ones
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Must complete all pending repays or wait for sDAI value recovery
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All sDAI yield staking operations for affected accounts

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverts on new stake attempts for sDAI strategy
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Failed transactions to sDAI yield strategy

threats.title: Dust Debt Amounts Round to Zero in Health Factor Calculation Enabling Invisible Debt Accumulation
threats.hypothesis_description: In GenericLogic._getUserERC20DebtInBaseCurrency, the calculation `(assetPrice * userTotalDebt) / (10 ** assetData.underlyingDecimals)` performs integer division that truncates small debt amounts to zero. For tokens with high decimals (18) and low unit prices, very small borrow positions can have their debt value round to zero in the health factor calculation. An attacker could open many small positions across groups that individually round to zero, accumulating real debt that is invisible to the health factor check.
threats.investigation_rationale: While each individual dust position is negligible, the systematic exploitation across many groups and assets could accumulate meaningful untracked debt. The health factor would show infinity while actual debt exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: return userTotalDebt / (10 ** assetData.underlyingDecimals); integer division truncates small values to 0

preconditions: Asset with 18 decimals and relatively low price in base currency
Borrow positions small enough that price * amount / 10^18 < 1
Multiple such positions across different groups/assets

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Integer division with no rounding up for debt calculation (conservative for collateral but dangerous for debt)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent for any supported asset where dust amounts round to zero

operational_questions: For each configured asset, what is the minimum borrow amount that registers as non-zero debt?
How many dust positions can be opened before gas costs exceed profit?
Does the borrow cap validation also suffer from similar rounding?
validation_requirements: Calculate dust threshold for each asset based on price and decimals
Test health factor with many dust borrows
Verify whether dust debt can be withdrawn without health factor penalty

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify assets where minimum non-zero base currency value requires significant borrow
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Open many dust borrow positions just below the rounding threshold
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Health factor remains infinity despite actual accumulated debt
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Withdraw collateral while holding invisible debt, defaulting on positions
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - single account with many small borrows

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Health factor monitoring undermined for dust positions
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Small but systematic bad debt accumulation
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Dust positions may not be economically liquidatable
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All cross-margin borrow operations with very small amounts

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - requires understanding of decimal precision

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Accounts with many minimum-size borrows across groups
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Health factor infinity despite non-zero borrow balances
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Systematic small borrows below detection threshold

threats.title: YieldRegistry Rescue Function Enables Arbitrary Calls From Any Yield Account Draining User Staked Assets
threats.hypothesis_description: YieldRegistry.rescue(yieldAccount, target, data) allows any POOL_ADMIN to execute arbitrary function calls from any YieldAccount via the registry's onlyRegistry modifier. Since YieldAccounts hold user staked assets (stETH, eETH, sDAI), a compromised or malicious pool admin can use rescue to transfer all staked assets out of every user's yield account. The function has no restrictions on target or data, and no timelock or multi-sig requirement at the contract level.
threats.investigation_rationale: While POOL_ADMIN is a trusted role, the rescue function provides god-mode access to all user staked assets. The deployment scripts show POOL_ADMIN is initially set to the deployer address with no timelock.
deployment_assumptions: POOL_ADMIN key is secured; no timelock wraps the ACL role
threats.config_evidence: DeployPoolFull.s.sol: aclManager.addPoolAdmin(deployer) grants deployer direct admin access

proof_of_signal.pattern: onlyPoolAdmin guard; YieldAccount(payable(yieldAccount)).rescue(target, data) executes arbitrary call from yield account

preconditions: POOL_ADMIN private key compromised or malicious admin action
Yield accounts hold staked assets (stETH, eETH, sDAI)
No external timelock or multi-sig protects the POOL_ADMIN role

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Arbitrary external call capability from user-controlled asset storage
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Complete admin discretion over all user yield positions
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - as long as yield accounts hold assets

operational_questions: Is the POOL_ADMIN role behind a timelock or multi-sig?
Can rescue be called on yield accounts that are actively staked?
Total value at risk across all yield accounts?
validation_requirements: Verify POOL_ADMIN key management setup
Test rescue extracting stETH from an active yield position
Assess whether rescue bypasses any accounting checks

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call rescue for each yield account: rescue(account, stETH, transfer(attacker, balance))
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All user staked assets (stETH, eETH, sDAI) drained from yield accounts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users cannot repay debts (no yield to claim), creating cascading bad debt

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete drain of all user yield positions
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All yield staking strategies become insolvent
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Funds unrecoverable if transferred to external address
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All yield staking across Lido, EtherFi, and sDAI

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic - standard ERC20 transfer calls via rescue

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Rescue calls from YieldRegistry to yield accounts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Yield account balances dropping without corresponding unstake/repay

threats.title: Fee Collection Order of Operations Allows Interest Accrual After Fee Reset Creating Minor Fee Leakage
threats.hypothesis_description: In PoolLogic.executeCollectFeeToTreasury, accruedFee is read then zeroed BEFORE calling InterestLogic.updateInterestIndexs. The updateInterestIndexs call accrues new fees via _accrueFeeToTreasury into the now-zeroed accruedFee. The collected amount uses the OLD accruedFee multiplied by the CURRENT normalizedIncome (post-update). While functionally this captures the old fees correctly, the newly accrued fees from the same updateInterestIndexs call are stored but not collected until the next fee collection. This creates a timing dependency where the order of fee collection relative to other operations affects how much fee is collected, potentially leaving small amounts perpetually uncollected if collection frequency is low.
threats.investigation_rationale: While not a direct fund loss, this creates an asymmetry in fee accounting that compounds over time. The fee collection process should ideally be atomic - either collect everything including newly accrued fees, or clearly separate the accrual and collection phases.

proof_of_signal.pattern: accruedFee read, set to 0, THEN updateInterestIndexs called which accrues new fees to zeroed field; amountToCollect based on old fee only

preconditions: Non-zero accruedFee exists for the asset
Significant time has passed since last interest update (new fee would accrue)
Admin calls executeCollectFeeToTreasury

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Fee zeroed before interest update; new fees accrued to zero field not included in collection
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Fee collection is admin-triggered, timing affects total collected

operational_questions: What is the magnitude of fees left behind per collection?
Does the next collection pick up previously uncollected fees?
Could an attacker time operations to minimize protocol fee revenue?
validation_requirements: Compare fee collection with updateInterestIndexs before vs after fee reset
Calculate fee leakage per collection cycle
Verify accumulated fees are eventually collected

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: This is a design inefficiency rather than an attack vector
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Each fee collection misses the fee accrued during the update in the same transaction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The missed fee is small (one block's worth of accrual) and picked up next collection
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Net impact is delayed fee collection, not permanent loss

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Treasury receives slightly delayed fee income
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Fees are eventually collected; no permanent loss

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Non-zero accruedFee remaining after fee collection
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Fee collection amounts slightly less than expected
